text: Welcome today. We're going to be doing a laparoscopic liver resection today. This is a young lady who's sixty-four years old. She has liver disease and we're going to be resecting a hepatocellular carcinoma that is in her left lateral section. So we'll be showing you some of the steps of laparoscopic liver surgery in a pure technique. This is the HDI harmonic. This is advanced hemostasis. The harmonic HDI has provided us with a much better curb tip, longer tip. It is still the same efficacy as far as vascular or the amount of division that you can do with a large vessel, but it actually is quicker. So the software is improved such that it will cut through vessels at a much quicker pace. So currently, this is the young lady who had a very old cholecystectomy. She has very significant adhesions and a very large previous incision on her right upper quadrant.
The resident is dissecting around the vena cava, with the left lateral section of the liver visibly mobilized.

The surgeon identifies and points out the phrenic veins as they enter the left side, noting their size. The view shifts to the posterior area of the liver, where the surgeon and resident work to detach attachments near the caudate lobe. The liver appears cirrhotic, likely due to the patient's Hepatitis C and B.

The surgeon uses the harmonic HDI device to efficiently dissect and cut through the liver parenchyma. The flat blade and pressure distribution allow for effective cutting at a higher efficiency level. The dissection proceeds for the first two centimeters on both the top and bottom sides of the liver tissue.
The surgeon meticulously closes the harmonic blade while advancing through the liver tissue, carefully assessing for any resistance that may indicate atypical vasculature or biliary structures.
